summarization.a: Optimized structures for H₂ dissociative chemisorption on the Ti-terminated TiN (111) surface, showing H₂ adsorption, H–H bond dissociation, and adsorption of H atoms at three-fold hollow sites formed by Ti atoms.
summarization.b: Optimized structures for N₂ dissociative chemisorption on the Ti-terminated TiN (111) surface, showing adsorption, N≡N bond dissociation, and N atoms occupying the same three-fold hollow Ti sites. The figure highlights the high reactivity of the Ti-terminated (111) surface compared to TiN (001).

d.question: Explain why the Ti-terminated TiN (111) surface exhibits higher reactivity for H₂ and N₂ dissociation than the TiN (001) surface.
d.answer: The Ti-terminated TiN (111) surface has more dangling bonds per Ti atom compared to TiN (001), which increases surface reactivity. This facilitates H–H and N–N bond dissociation with lower activation energies and higher adsorption energies, making dissociative chemisorption energetically favorable. Understanding this surface reactivity is important for predicting catalytic behavior and designing materials for surface reactions.

b.question: How do the activation energies for H₂ and N₂ dissociation compare on Ti-terminated TiN (111) versus TiN (001)?
b.answer: The dissociation of both H₂ and N₂ is more facile on Ti-terminated TiN (111) due to smaller activation energies (0.11 eV for H₂, 0.92 eV for N₂) compared to TiN (001).

a.question: Does the dissociative chemisorption of H₂ on Ti-terminated TiN (111) involve H–H bond breaking before adsorption?

c.question: List the key structural stages and adsorption sites for H₂ and N₂ dissociative chemisorption on Ti-terminated TiN (111).
c.answer: Initial state (IS) – molecular adsorption, Transition state (TS) – bond stretching/dissociation, Final state (FS) – dissociated H or N atoms adsorbed, Adsorption site – three-fold hollow site formed by Ti atoms
